items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: What does in-order traversal of a binary search tree produce?
items.misconception: Nodes in the order they were inserted.
items.why: Students forget the BST property: left subtree less than node less than right subtree. In-order (left, node, right) therefore visits values in sorted order - regardless of insertion order.

items.q: What does a left shift by 1 do to a binary number?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Multiplies by 2. “Divides by 2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: By De Morgan's law, NOT(A OR B) equals what?
items.why: It's NOT A AND NOT B. “NOT A OR NOT B” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is binary 0000 0101 left-shifted by 2 in denary?
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
